title: Personal Metrics Dashboard

content.question: Do you have a personal dashboard, or are you guessing at your own progress?
sop: Pick 3-5 metrics that actually reflect your progress, not vanity numbers, ones you'd act on if they moved
Set up one place to log them (spreadsheet, app, notebook), consistency matters more than the tool
Update the numbers on a fixed cadence, daily for some, weekly for others
Rate your current visibility into each metric 1-10 before you start tracking (see calculation, page 6)
Review the trend monthly, not just the latest number, one data point is noise
content.explainer: Insight doesn't come from having more experience or better instincts, it comes from being able to actually see the pattern, which requires the number to exist somewhere outside your memory. A dashboard doesn't create the progress; it just makes progress (or its absence) impossible to lie to yourself about.
questions: Do you have a personal dashboard, or are you guessing at your own progress?
If someone asked you right now for your top 3 numbers, weight, savings rate, whatever matters to you, could you answer without checking?
What's a metric you assume is fine because you haven't actually looked at it in months?
content.equation_example: Visibility rated 2 out of 10 (rarely check your numbers) produces vague, guessy insight. Visibility rated 8 out of 10 (checked weekly, trends visible) produces specific, actionable insight. Higher visibility means you catch drift early; lower visibility means you only notice a problem once it's already large.
content.closing_statement: What you don't track, you're only guessing about.

caption:
Principle Nº 218: Personal Metrics Dashboard

Track core measures transparently. Insight doesn't come from having more experience or better instincts, it comes from being able to actually see the pattern, which requires the number to exist somewhere outside your memory.

Ask yourself: Do you have a personal dashboard, or are you guessing at your own progress?
